coredata.dc:title: Spermidine-capped carbon dots as potent antimicrobial nanomaterials against Escherichia coli
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Tianqi
Wu, Yue
Peng, Zeyu
Ban, Qingfeng
Wang, Mingyang
Cheng, Jianjun
Jiang, Yunqing
coredata.dc:description: The food industry must prevent food contamination caused by pathogenic Escherichia coli, as they pose a severe public health threat globally, hence the need to explore new antimicrobial materials. In this paper, spermidine capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction, and the antibacterial effects against E. coli and multi-drug resistant E. coli (MREC) was determined, so as to explore their potential application as antibacterial nanomaterials. The minimum inhibitory concentration values of S-PCDs against E. coli and MREC were 64 μg/mL and 128 μg/mL, respectively, showing strong antibacterial activity. The antibacterial zone was increased significantly with the increasing content of S-PCDs (p < 0.05). The results of bacteriostatic curve and bactericidal curve of S-PCDs on E. coli and MREC also showed that S-PCDs had a concentration-dependent inhibition on bacteria. In addition, the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism indicated that the antibacterial action of S-PCDs may involve cell membrane damage and reactive oxygen species production. During 30 days of exposure, S-PCDs showed persistent antibacterial effect against both E. coli and MREC without detectable resistance, especially without triggering secondary resistance in MREC. S-PCDs has potential application value in foodborne diseases caused by complex bacteria and even drug-resistant bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antibacterial activity
Escherichia coli
Multi-drug resistant Escherichia coli
Resistance development

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth curve and time-kill kinetics analysis
The damage of membrane permeability and integrity determination
Confocal laser scanning microscope observe bacterial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial activity of S-PCDs. Inhibition zone of S-PCDs on (a) E. coli and (b) MREC and corresponding (c) inhibition zone diameter and inhibition rate. Data were presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). Superscripts with different letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05), * p < 0.05. (d, e) Bacteriostatic curve and (f, g) bactericidal curve of different concentrations of S-PCDs against E. coli and MREC at different periods.

Microscope observe bacteria structure. TEM (a1-a4) ( × 15 k) and SEM (b1-b4) ( × 20 k) images of E. coli and MREC without treatment (a1, a3 and b1, b3) or with treatment of S-PCDs (a2, a4 and b2, b4).

Cell membrane damage. (a) Zeta-potential of S-PCDs, E. coli and MREC (with or without S-PCDs treatment). Uptake of PI in (b) E. coli and (c) MREC. Effect of S-PCDs on the extracellular protein content of (d) E. coli, and (e) MREC. Effect of S-PCDs on the nucleic acid content of (f) E. coli, and (g) MREC. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

SYTO 9/PI dual-staining assay to examine the integrity of bacterial membrane. CLSM images of E. coli and MREC (with or without S-PCDs treatment) were stained by SYTO 9/PI dual fluorescence staining. All bacteria alive and dead were stained by SYTO 9 showing green fluorescent intensity. Only dead bacteria can be stained by PI emitting red fluorescent intensity. For SYTO 9: λex = 488 nm; for PI: λex = 552 nm. The scale bar is 75 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

ROS generation. (a) The generation of ROS in E. coli and MREC treated with and without S-PCDs by using the dye DCFH-DA. (b) Comparison on relative expression level of dmpI, narJ, and nark in E. coli after treatment with S-PCDs. *** p < 0.001 (vs the control group).

Schematic illustration on the synthesis of S-PCDs and antibacterial mechanism of the S-PCDs. (a) Polyethyleneimine and spermidine were used as raw materials to synthesize spermidine-capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) by hydrothermal reaction. (b) Antibacterial mechanism of the S-PCDs.

Bacterial resistance of S-PCDs. Resistance development of (a) E. coli and (b) MREC during 30 days in the presence of S-PCDs. Relative bacterial numbers of (c) E. coli and (d) MREC (30 repeatedly passages) colonies treated with S (4 MIC) and S-PCDs (4 MIC) on the LB agar plates successively (** p < 0.01). All cells were subjected to 24 h of incubation at 37 °C.
MIC of spermidine, PEI, S-PCDs, and kanamycin against 13 strains bacteria.

head.ce:title: Spermidine-capped carbon dots as potent antimicrobial nanomaterials against Escherichia coli

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. No. 600, Changjiang Road, Harbin, 150000, China.
Corresponding author. No. 600, Changjiang Road, Harbin, 150000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The food industry must prevent food contamination caused by pathogenic Escherichia coli, as they pose a severe public health threat globally, hence the need to explore new antimicrobial materials. In this paper, spermidine capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction, and the antibacterial effects against E. coli and multi-drug resistant E. coli (MREC) was determined, so as to explore their potential application as antibacterial nanomaterials. The minimum inhibitory concentration values of S-PCDs against E. coli and MREC were 64 μg/mL and 128 μg/mL, respectively, showing strong antibacterial activity. The antibacterial zone was increased significantly with the increasing content of S-PCDs (p < 0.05). The results of bacteriostatic curve and bactericidal curve of S-PCDs on E. coli and MREC also showed that S-PCDs had a concentration-dependent inhibition on bacteria. In addition, the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism indicated that the antibacterial action of S-PCDs may involve cell membrane damage and reactive oxygen species production. During 30 days of exposure, S-PCDs showed persistent antibacterial effect against both E. coli and MREC without detectable resistance, especially without triggering secondary resistance in MREC. S-PCDs has potential application value in foodborne diseases caused by complex bacteria and even drug-resistant bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogens, which posing a serious threat to public health, are one of the most important concerns of the food industry (Park, Lee, & Baik, 2022). Escherichia coli is a typical foodborne pathogen and is considered the third leading cause of disease in the world (Shi et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2019). It is frequently found in meat, dairy products, raw vegetables, seafood and other foods, posing a serious health threat to food processors and consumers (Park et al., 2022). Antibiotics are an effective treatment, however more and more bacteria have developed antimicrobial resistance (AMR) due to the heavy use of antibiotics (H. Zheng et al., 2019). At present, antimicrobial peptides, probiotics and nanomaterials have been designed by researchers to inhibit or reduce serious bacterial infections (Ali et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2022; Kaiyuan Zheng, Setyawati, Leong, & Xie, 2018). Although many nanomaterials such as silver nanoparticles have been proven to have effective antibacterial activity against bacteria (Jia et al., 2021). However, these antibacterial nanomaterials may also cause AMR (Nino-Martinez, Salas Orozco, Martinez-Castanon, Torres Mendez, & Ruiz, 2019), and the potential toxicity limits their application in the food industry. Therefore, exploring low toxicity antimicrobials that do not lead to bacterial resistance is a challenging task.

Carbon dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials among various antibacterial nanomaterials, have attracted more and more attention due to their small size (diameter less than 10 nm), convenient preparation and adjustable structure (J. Yang et al., 2016). More importantly, the good biocompatibility and low toxicity of CDs enable them to be better used in the food field. Heteroatom-doped CDs were prepared using sour whey as raw material by hydrothermal method. CDs have good antibacterial and antioxidant ability and low cytotoxicity, which can fight foodborne pathogens and be used in food preservation (Mahmoud et al., 2023). Positively charged carbon quantum dot with broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity were prepared, which can effectively inhibit drug-resistant bacteria without inducing detectable drug resistance (Hao et al., 2021; Roy, Kim, Paoprasert, Park, & In, 2015). Usually, raw materials containing a large number of amino groups, such as polyethyleneimine, will form a large number of positive charges on the surface of the CDs prepared (Hao et al., 2021). This positively charged CDs can bind more easily to the negative sites on the surface of bacteria, severely damaging their cell membranes and causing their death (Travlou et al., 2018). In addition, spermidine can reduce the minimum inhibitory concentration of lactam antibiotics and other types of antibiotics in a variety of strains, especially E. coli (Kwon and Lu., 2007). Highly cationic carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction using spermidine, o-phenylenediamine and α-lipoic acid, which can effectively inhibit seven strains of bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli (F. Cui et al., 2021). Jian et al. (2017) synthesized N-doped CDs with a high positive charge (+45 mV) using spermidine, and these CDs showed effective antimicrobial activity against S. aureus, E. coli and various AMR bacteria. Although the antibacterial activity of CDs has been confirmed, the mechanism of CDs against E. coli and whether CDs can lead to the evolution of bacterial resistance is still unclear.

In this study, spermidine-capped CDs (S-PCDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method. Preliminary results showed that the S-PCDs have a diameter of 6.87 nm and contain amino groups on the surface with a positive charge (+47.06 mV). The antibacterial activity and mechanism of S-PCDs was investigated using pathogenic Escherichia coli, and multi-drug resistant E. coli as model microorganisms. The effects of S-PCDs on the evolution of AMR in drug-resistant bacteria was investigated at sub-minimum inhibitory concentration exposure. This work aimed to provide a new effective control of E. coli and even drug-resistant E. coli in food field to replace antibiotics and avoid drug resistance. The synthetic CDs have the potential to be used as antibacterial agents in food formulations, as well as nanomaterials for the development of composite polymers for food packaging.

Polyethyleneimine (PEI10000) was purchased from Macrlin Biochemical Technology (Shanghai, China). Spermidine was provided by Yuanye Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Luria Bertani (LB) broth was obtained from Obo Star Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). LIVE/DEAD BacLight Bacterial Viability Kit (Cat. no. L7012) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Shanghai, China). NCTC L929 fibroblast cells and L929 medium were purchased from Procell Life Science & Technology (Wuhan, China).

Professor Fengling Bai and Defu Zhang from Bohai University donated Escherichia coli and multi-drug resistant E. coli (MREC) resistant to azithromycin, gentamicin and tetracycline, respectively. E. coli and MREC were cultured at 37 °C for 24 h, respectively. After sub-cultured twice in LB medium, the bacterial suspension was diluted to 106–107 CFU/mL in sterile medium and stored at 4 °C for use (Zhang et al., 2023).

S-PCDs was synthesized by two hydrothermal synthesis methods with some modified (Jian et al., 2017). PEI (10 mg/mL) was added to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 220 °C for 2 h to synthesize P-CDs. After cooling to room temperature, spermidine (2.5 g, mass ratio to PEI = 1.0) was added and heated at 220 °C for 3 h to synthesize S-PCDs. The suspension of S-PCDs was centrifuged at 10,500×g for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The obtained S-PCDs solution was dialyzed with deionized water in a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag for 5 h, and the dialysate was changed every 1 h. Finally, the prepared S-PCDs were stored at 4 °C or vacuum freeze-dried for use.

The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined by the two-fold serial dilution technique with 96-well microplate (X. Wang, He, Yuan, Deng, & Zhang, 2021). Diluted solutions of S-PCDs were prepared with LB broth. S-PCDs (200 μL, 256 μg/mL) were added to well 1, and 100 μL LB broth was added to the other wells, respectively. Then, S-PCDs of 100 μL in well 1 was continuously diluted downward, so that the final concentrations of S-PCDs were 128, 64, 32, 8, 4, 2, and 1 μg/mL, respectively. 10 μL of bacterial suspension approximately 106 CFU/mL was added to each well. LB broth with the same amount of sterile water was used as the control group. The 96-well microplate was then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h and examined for the growth.

Antibacterial activity was determined by the Oxford cup punch method. 1 mL 106 CFU/mL bacterial suspension was added to 25 mL medium, and then poured into a sterile Petri dish with Oxford cups. After the medium solidified, the Oxford cup was taken out and 180 μL of 1, 2 and 4 MIC of S-PCDs was added, and then cultured at 37 °C for 24 h to observe the diameter of inhibition zone. Sterile water was used as a blank control. After measuring the size of the inhibition circle, the bacteriostasis rate was calculated by the following formula (T. Cui et al., 2020):
(1)
bacteriostasis
rate
( % )
=
(
D
s a m p l e
−
D
c o n t r o l
)
/
D
s a m p l e
× 100 %
where D
sample is diameter of bacteriostatic circle of treatment, D
control is diameter of bacteriostatic circle of control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth curve and time-kill kinetics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The bactericidal and bacteriostatic curves of S-PCDs were evaluated according to previously study (Shi et al., 2021). The bacteria were incubated in the LB broth at 37 °C for 24 h and diluted to a final concentration of 106 CFU/mL. S-PCDs was added into the bacterial suspension of each tube so that the final concentration were 1/16, 1/8, 1/4, 1/2, and 1 MIC. Samples without S-PCDs were used as a negative control and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The above cultures (180 μL) were placed in a 96-well microplate every 2 h, and cell growth was measured at OD 600 nm using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad, USA). As for bactericidal curves analysis, Bacteria cultures of 106 CFU/mL were exposed to 1, 2, 4, and 8 MIC of S-PCDs and cultures without S-PCDs as negative control, the co-cultures was cultured at 37 °C. After 2 h of incubation, 1 mL of culture was diluted with LB broth. The dilution culture (100 μL) was spread on the LB solid medium and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h for colony counting.
The scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) observation were used to observe the E. coli images (F. Cui et al., 2021).

1/2 MIC S-PCDs was added to 200 μL prepared bacterial suspension and cultured at 37 °C for 12 h. Bacterial cells were obtained by centrifugation at 500×g for 15 min, and then the cells were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.2, 0.2 mol/L) and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution at 4 °C for 12 h. The bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS and then dehydrated with 30, 50, 70, 90, and 100% ethanol solutions for 15 min, respectively. The strains were sputter-coated with gold, and their morphological and surface characteristics were observed using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

Bacteria were cultured, cleaned and fixed as above. The bacterial suspension was diluted to the appropriate concentration and placed on a copper grid for adsorption for 1 h and then dried. The dried samples were put into transmission electron microscope (H-700, Hitachi, Japan) to observe the structure of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The damage of membrane permeability and integrity determination
ce:section.ce:para: PI (propyl iodide) uptake: The PI uptake test was conducted according to Shi et al. (2021). After inoculation, the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 6000×g at 4 °C for 15 min. The bacteria cells were washed three times with PBS (0.2 m mol/L, pH 7.2), and resuspended with the same PBS (10 mL) to a final concentration of 106 CFU/mL 1 MIC S-PCDs was added and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The culture was incubated with PI at 37 °C for 10 min in the dark. Fluorescence was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 495 nm and an emission wavelength of 500–700 nm, and an excitation wavelength of 495 nm and an emission wavelength of 500–700 nm. Sample without treatment of S-PCDs was used as a negative control.

Relative amount of extracellular nucleic acids: The relative amount of extracellular nucleic acids of E. coli and MREC was measured according to the method of Chen and Cooper (2002). S-PCDs were added to 20 mL of prepared bacterial suspension at a concentration of 1 MIC, and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Sterile water was used as a control. The bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 1000 g every 2 h for 15 min. The absorbance of the collected supernatant was analyzed at 260 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Relative amount of extracellular proteins: Referring to the previous published paper (X. Wang et al., 2021), a BCA kit was used to detect the effect of S-PCDs on the relative amount of extracellular proteins in E. coli and MREC. The prepared bacterial suspension (106 CFU/mL) was added to LB broth with 1 MIC of S-PCDs, and cultured at 37 °C for 12 h. The co-cultures were centrifuged at 1000×g every 2 h for 15 min. The protein content of the supernatant was determined by BCA kit. Sterile water was as a control, and the change curve of protein content was drawn.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal laser scanning microscope observe bacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: The method of Yang et al. (2021) and Yu et al. (2019) was followed (H. Yang et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2019). Bacterial suspension (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was treated with S-PCDs and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The cultures were centrifuged at 10,000×g for 10 min and then suspended in 8.5% normal saline. The bacterial culture (1 mL) was washed twice, 3 μL SYTO 9 and PI were added and incubated in the dark for 15 min. Fluorescence images of bacterial samples were recorded using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica TCS SP5, Heidelberg, Germany).

Bacterial cells were diluted to a final concentration of 106 CFU/mL. Bacterial suspension and S-PCDs with final concentrations of 1, 2 and 4 MIC were added to each flask and co-incubated for 12 h. Bacterial cells were centrifuged at 6000×g for 5 min and then resuspended in the same volume of PBS (pH = 7.2, 0.2 mol/L). 10 μL 2′, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH−DA, 10 μmol/L) was added to the suspension. After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the bacterial cells were washed three times to remove the excess fluorescent probes. Untreated bacteria were used as a control. The fluorescence intensity was detected at an excitation wavelength of 484 nm and an emission wavelength of 525 nm. ROS production ability in each group was expressed using a relative ROS level (Yu et al., 2019):
(2)
R O S
s a m p l e
/
R O S
c o n t r o l

Total RNA was extracted after S-PCDs and E. coli were co-cultured at 37 °C for 24 h and washed with sterile PBS for 3 times. Gene 9600 Quantitative PCR Istrument (Bori, Hangzhou, China) was used for quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR). Primer was synthesized by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) and shown in Table 1 . The gene gyrB was used as an internal reference gene, and gene expression differences were calculated by 2−△△Ct method (T. Cui et al., 2020; K. Zheng, Setyawati, Leong, & Xie, 2021).

The potential for antimicrobial resistance development of S-PCDs against E. coli and MREC were determined using a previous method (H. Yang et al., 2021; H. Zheng, Ji, Roy, Gao, & Li, 2019). In detail, 1% of E. coli and MREC cells were added to sterile LB broth containing 1/2 MIC of S-PCDs, respectively, and then cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. The proliferation of bacterial cells was calculated by detecting the absorbance of the co-cultures. When the bacterial cells adapted to the current S-PCDs concentration and the cell proliferation rate was >85% (Eq. (3)), the treatment dose was doubled, otherwise the S-PCDs concentration was sustained for a further period (24 h). The above testing process was repeated every 24 h to achieve a gradient increment of S-PCDs concentration. Spermidine and the raw material of S-PCDS were used as a negative control. After 30 generations, the total number of colonies after treatment with spermidine (4 MIC) and S-PCDs (4 MIC) were determined by LB plates, respectively.
(3)
Cell
growth =
(
A 1
−
A 2
)
/
(
A 3
−
A 4
)
Where A
1 − A
4 is the absorbance of bacteria treated with S-PCDs, the S-PCDs in LB broth, the untreated bacteria, and the LB broth, respectively.

Statistical analyses were conducted using SPSS 19.0 (IBM SPSS Statistics) software. All experiments were obtained from at least three times and the results were recorded as mean ± standard deviation (SD). A significance level was 0.05.

The MIC of S-PCDs against 13 strains Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria were determined, and the results are shown in Table 2 . The S-PCDs had antibacterial effect on both G+ and G– bacteria, and the MIC against G+ bacteria was less than that against G– bacteria. S-PCDs showed the best antibacterial effect against typical Gram-positive strain Staphylococcus aureus with MIC of 16 μg/mL. S-PCDs were effective against multidrug-resistant strains, with MICs of S-PCDs against multidrug-resistant E. coli (MREC) and Vibrio Parahemolyticus (MRVP) were 128 and 256 μg/mL. The MIC of S-PCDs against E. coli was 64 μg/mL, which did not reach the antimicrobial activity of kanamycin (MIC of 4 μg/mL), but was significantly higher than that of the two ingredients. In addition, due to the resistance of MREC to kanamycin, its MIC was 256 μg/mL, but the MIC of S-PCDs against MERC was reduced by twice. As shown in Fig. 1 , both E. coli and MREC treated with S-PCDs (MIC, 2 MIC and 4 MIC) produced distinct inhibition zones with diameters larger than 12 mm (Fig. 1a and b). In addition, the inhibition circle increased significantly with increasing S-PCDs concentration (p < 0.05). After 4 MIC S-PCDs treatment, the inhibition rate of E. coli reached 63.08%, which was higher than that of MERC (64.44%) (Fig. 1c).

The bacteriostatic curve and bactericidal curve of S-PCDs against E. coli and MREC were evaluated. The time-dependent bacteriostatic curve of S-PCDs against bacteria at 1/16−1 MIC was evaluated by measuring the OD600 value of the cultures (Fig. 1d and e). The OD600 values of E. coli and MREC treated with different concentrations of S-PCDs were lower than those of the control group, indicating that S-PCDs had an inhibitory effect on bacteria. Meanwhile, the OD600 value decreased with increasing S-PCDs concentration, demonstrating that S-PCDs had a concentration-dependent inhibitory effect on bacteria. The OD600 value of E. coli increased exponentially after 4–10 h of growth. The growth of MREC was relatively slow in the initial stage compared to that of E. coli. 1/2 MIC S-PCDs inhibited both E. coli and MREC, but the inhibitory effect on E. coli was stronger than that on MREC.

Secondly, colony count was measured after different concentrations of S-PCDs acted on E. coli and MREC for different time to evaluate the bactericidal effect of S-PCDs on them (Fig. 1f and g). The two strains showed a continuous downward trend within 12 h when treated with MIC and higher concentration of S-PCDs. The bactericidal rates of E. coli and MREC were 94.14% and 92.42%, respectively, after treatment with 1 MIC S-PCDs for 4 h. The bactericidal rates of E. coli and MREC treated with 2 MIC S-PCDs for 4 h were 98.56% and 97.14%, respectively. 4 MIC S-PCDs could completely kill E. coli after 10 h of treatment. In addition, 8 MIC S-PCDs could completely kill E. coli and MREC within 10 h. The bactericidal and bacteriostatic curves showed that S-PCDs had good antibacterial activity against both E. coli and MREC in a concentration-dependent. In addition, S-PCDs showed stronger antibacterial activity against E. coli, which was consistent with the results of the bacteriostatic rate of the Oxford cup method.

The results of FTIR and XPS showed that the relative contents of main elements C, O and N of S-PCDs were 76.79%, 11.9% and 11.31%, respectively. The surface of S-PCDs contains a large number of amino groups, which may make it carry a large number of positive charges, thus enhancing its antibacterial activity (Hao et al., 2021). In addition, TEM results showed that the mean diameter of S-PCDs was 6.87 nm. Studies showed that nanoparticles have size-dependent bactericidal effects (Shekhar Agnihotri et al., 2014), and CDs with diameters of 1–10 nm have the best bactericidal effect because they are able to enter bacterial cells through the broken cell membrane (Shang, Nienhaus, & Nienhaus, 2014). These properties of S-PCDs are closely related to their antibacterial mechanism.

The results of TEM image showed that untreated E. coli had clear bacterial morphology with intact and smooth cell membranes, while MREC showed short and coarse bacterial morphology with clearly visible flagella (Fig. 2 a1, a3). After treatment with S-PCDs, the cell membrane of both strains became irregular with surface damage and leakage of intracellular contents. In addition, part of the flagella of MREC was missing and the bacterium surface became rough (Fig. 2a2, a4). Moreover, SEM images of untreated bacterial cells also showed clear edges and smooth cell membrane surfaces (Fig. 2b1, b3). However, after 24 h of S-PCDs incubation, not only the number of bacteria decreased, but also the bacterial cells surface showed obvious folds, and many bacteria showed membrane defects and leakage of cell contents (Fig. 2b2, b4). The results suggested that S-PCDs adsorbed on the bacterial surface through physical interaction, causing cell membrane damage, leading to leakage of intracellular substance and physiological dysfunction, and ultimately leading to bacterial death. This result was also consistent with the antimicrobial mechanism described in previous studies (Hao et al., 2021; Roy et al., 2015).

The surface capping properties of CDs, especially the surface charge, are key factors affecting their antibacterial performance. The zeta potentials of E. coli and MREC were −14.37 and −12.25 mV, respectively, whereas that of S-PCDs was +47.06 mV, and increased with the addition of 1/2 MIC S-PCDs (Fig. 3 a). The results showed that the positive charge on the surface of S-PCDs facilitates the binding of bacteria to the negative charge on the surface via electrostatic interaction, which further destroyed the integrity of the cell membrane and physiological metabolic activities of bacteria, and ultimately led to bacterial death. Electrostatic interactions and small size generally promote the entry of S-PCDs into bacteria, and this interaction process may not lead to bacterial death, thus realizing a broad spectrum of antibacterial capacity (Xin et al., 2019).

PI can only enter the bacterial cell membrane after it has been disrupted and bind to nucleic acid to emit fluorescence signals. Therefore, the change in fluorescence intensity after PI treatment can characterize the damage to the cell membrane (Shi et al., 2021). The fluorescence intensity is shown in Fig. 3b and c. In the control group, the fluorescence intensity of PI remained unchanged with the extension of time. After 4 h of treatment with S-PCDs, PI value increased significantly with the extension of incubation time (p < 0.001). Both E. coli and MREC showed a similar trend. It is possible that S-PCDs treatment leads to alteration of cell membrane permeability, resulting in bacterial lysis and allowing PI to enter the cytoplasm and bind to DNA. This was also consistent with the results of Zheng et al. (2018) that CDs can tightly adhere and accumulate on the surface of the cell membrane, altering the permeability of the cell membrane and ultimately killing the bacteria.

The ability of CDs to damage cell membranes is also reflected in the leakage of bacterial contents, such as proteins and nucleic acids (Shen et al., 2015). The results of extracellular protein concentration and nucleic acid (DNA and RNA) release from E. coli and MREC after treatment with S-PCDs are shown in Fig. 3d–g. The extracellular soluble protein concentrations of E. coli and MREC treated with S-PCDs were significantly increased (p < 0.001) compared with that of the control group (Fig. 3d and e). The increase in protein concentration was gradually stabilized after 6 h of treatment. The protein concentrations of E. coli and MREC treated with S-PCDs for 12 h were 7.7 and 7.6 times higher than that of the control group, respectively. The results of nucleotide release were similar to the trend of protein leakage. The OD260 nm of E. coli and MERC increased rapidly and then gradually stabilized (Fig. 3f and g). These results suggested that S-PCDs may disrupt membrane integrity and cause significant leakage of bacterial cell contents.

Cell membrane damage of E. coli and MREC after S-PCDs treatment were also observed by CLSM. As mentioned earlier, PI can only stain the DNA of bacteria after their cell membranes have been damaged, whereas SYTO 9 can stain the DNA of all bacteria, making SYTO9/PI double staining a reliable method for detecting bacterial membranes integrity (Pan et al., 2022). In the control group, there were few PI-stained bacteria and most of them showed normal growth state. After treatment with S-PCDs, the number of bacteria in the PI-stained area increased significantly, indicating that S-PCDS treatment induced the death of a large number of bacteria, resulting in the binding of PI with nucleic acid to produce strong red fluorescence (Fig. 4 ). SYTO 9/PI double fluorescence staining experiment have also confirmed that S-PCDs have good antibacterial activity by altering the cell membrane permeability.

The level of ROS production in the treated bacteria was detected and the results are showed in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing concentration of S-PCDs compared to the control group, indicating that S-PCDs stimulated ROS production (Fig. 5a). Consistent with antibacterial data, E. coli treated with S-PCDs produced higher levels of ROS than MREC. The ROS production increased with increasing concentration of S-PCDs and was significantly higher than that of the control group. The 1 MIC concentration of S-PCDs results in a 3.15-fold and 2.15-fold higher ROS production than that of the control group in E. coli and MREC, respectively (Fig. 5a). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of bacteria treated with S-PCDs increased with the increase of S-PCDs concentration, and the fluorescence intensity of E. coli and MREC treated with 2 MIC S-PCDs was 3.25 and 2.25 times higher than that of control group, respectively. When the concentration of S-PCDs was increased to 4 MIC, the fluorescence intensity of the two strains reached 4.15-fold and 3.15-fold of the control group, respectively, indicating that S-PCDs stimulated ROS production in a dose-dependent way. Recent studies have shown that the introduction of nanomaterials in bacteria can promote the disruption of metabolic balance of intracellular oxidation reactions and the production of large amounts of ROS by up-regulating genes encoding oxidase-stimulating enzymes (such as dmpI, narJ and nark) (Yu et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 5b, S-PCDs induced significant up-regulation of three genes (p < 0.001), which was consistent with ROS data. Oxidative stress is one of the key mechanisms of nanomaterial-mediated antibacterial action (G. Wang et al., 2017; Xin et al., 2019). The results suggested that S-PCDs entering the cells may cause metabolic imbalance by promoting oxidase-stimulating enzymes, resulting in an overall accumulation of intracellular ROS, leading to bacterial oxidative stress and ultimately bacterial death (Bardhan et al., 2019).

In summary, the potential antibacterial mechanism of S-PCDs was proposed (Fig. 6 ). Since the bacterial cell membrane is the first and most critical protective barrier of bacteria, S-PCDs first destroyed the bacterial membranes and thus act as bactericidal agents. One pathway is that S-PCDs, which are small in size and have an ultra-high positive surface charge, contact the cell membranes through electrostatic interactions, penetrated into the bacteria, and ultimately changing the cell membrane permeability a, which ultimately kills the bacteria. In addition, the ability of S-PCDs to damage the cell membranes was reflected in the leakage of bacterial contents, such as nucleic acids and proteins. In another pathway, S-PCDs produced large amounts of ROS, and elevated ROS levels were known to disrupt DNA, proteins and enzymes within cells, thereby interfering with normal bacterial metabolism and killing bacteria.

The possible induction of resistance in bacteria chronically exposed to S-PCDs was assessed for the first time. E. coli and MREC were serially cultured with S-PCDs at sub-MIC level for 30 days. During the 30 days exposure period, S-PCDs exhibited sustained antibacterial effects against E. coli and MREC without detectable resistance (Fig. 7 a). MREC, a strain that is resistant to multiple antibiotics, did not develop new resistance to S-PCDs (Fig. 7b). In contrast, the treatment of two raw materials, spermidine and polyethyleneimine, caused the bacteria to develop resistance. Spermidine showed a 64-fold increase in resistance to E. coli and MREC, and polyethyleneimine showed a128-fold increase in resistance to E. coli and MREC increased after incubation for 30 d. S-PCDs did not cause bacterial resistance, which might be related to the antibacterial mechanism of S-PCDs. The prepared S-PCDs do not bind certain bacterial subunits completely and specifically (H. Zheng et al., 2019), and this interaction may not induce AMR evolution in bacteria. The antimicrobial properties without detectable AMR evolution in bacteria suggests that S-PCDs may be a promising and effective antimicrobial strategy. The resistance and antimicrobial activity of S-PCDs were further evaluated by colony counting. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, the inhibitory effect of spermidine on E. coli and MREC were relatively weak at 4 MIC, which also indicated that the bacteria were obviously resistant to spermidine during long-term treatment. However, S-PCDs still had obvious antibacterial activity against these two strains, which may be closely related to its antibacterial mechanism.

In summary, spermidine-capped carbon dots (S-PCDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal reaction. E. coli and MREC were used as model strains to explore the potential application of S-PCDs as antibacterial nanoparticles. S-PCDs showed strong antibacterial activity against E. coli and MREC, and their antibacterial mechanisms included oxidative stress and membrane damage. More interestingly, no bacteria resistance was detected in E. coli and MREC after 30 generations of treatment. S-PCDs are simple and safe to prepare, and are expected to be an effective tool against foodborne pathogens. Although S-PCDs have been shown to have low cytotoxicity, their safety should be further verified in animal studies. In addition, as nanoparticles, the antibacterial mechanism of S-PCDs is also helpful to consider the interaction with carriers such as composite membranes or hydrogels to fight bacteria.

Tianqi Cui: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Data curation, Software. Yue Wu: Writing – review & editing, Software, Validation. Zeyu Peng: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Qingfeng Ban: Writing – review & editing, Software. Mingyang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software. Jianjun Cheng: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yunqing Jiang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
